summary: DHH explains his decision to buy a 17-inch iMac over the more powerful dual 1.25 GHz PowerMac, primarily because of noise issues. Apple's PowerMac line had been plagued by inconsistent fan and power supply quality, making purchasing one a gamble. Despite Apple's acknowledgment of the problem, their fixes had proven inadequate. DHH questions why Apple would risk the reputation of premium machines to save money on proper cooling when Dell had already solved the problem at lower price points.
key_points: The PowerMac's noise problems stemmed from poor-quality and inconsistent fans and power supplies across units
Buying a PowerMac was essentially a lottery — some units were quiet, others had headache-inducing whines
Apple's promised 'low noise' next-generation PowerMacs still turned out to be noisy
Dell had already solved the noise problem at much lower price points, making Apple's failure harder to justify
money_quotes: It's a $2,000 lottery that I'm unwilling to play.
What's even worse than poor-quality is inconsistent quality, which is exactly what the PowerMacs have.
What continues to escape me is why Apple will let the reputation of $2,000+ machines be tarnished to save a few measly bucks on proper fans.
Dell solved this problem a long time ago and their computers are virtually silent and they sell for much less than the offerings from Apple.
I'm disappointed, yet eagerly awaiting my 17" iMac.
key_insight: When a premium brand ships inconsistent hardware quality on its flagship products, even loyal customers will trade raw power for reliability and silence.
